name: Jerome Kern
description: American composer of musical theater and popular music (1885-1945)
abstract: Jerome David Kern (January 27, 1885 – November 11, 1945) was an American composer of musical theatre and popular music. One of the most important American theatre composers of the early 20th century, he wrote more than 700 songs, used in over 100 stage works, including such classics as "Ol' Man River", "Can't Help Lovin' Dat Man", "A Fin…

Roberta: Smoke Gets in Your Eyes
Music in the Air: The Song is You

The Night Boat: Don't You Want to Take Me
Very Warm for May: All the Things You Are

about_quotes.speaker: Big Nick Nicholas
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript mentions Jerome Kern only in a footnote as the composer of 'Yesterdays'; the interviewee discusses performing the song but does not mention any personal interaction or relationship with Kern.

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Haggart
about_quotes.evidence: Jerome Kern, “The Song Is You.” I mean it separates the men from the boys when you learn the release to “The Song Is You,” it goes into all kinds of strange levels, you know?
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Haggart interviewed by Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, September 22, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: I started getting really into these songs there, because you have these songs done in the jazz way by composers like Jerome Kern and Irving Berlin and Rogers and Hart
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Dave McKenna
about_quotes.evidence: They’re not put together like a Jerome Kern or an Irving Berlin song. You know, they had a form to them, a shape to them, it’s great.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave McKenna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: I mean where would Charlie Parker be without Jerome Kern you know? ... up to Jerome Kern and Cole Porter became more sophisticated.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Frishberg
about_quotes.evidence: Jerome Kern, who defined the chordal movement of bebop with that song... opened up jazz musicians’ ears all over the place
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Frishberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001
